items.q: How do you say 'my house' in French?
items.why: Students use mon as default. French possessives agree with the OBJECT's gender, not the owner. Maison is feminine, so MA maison. (Note: mon goes before feminine nouns starting with a vowel - mon amie.)

items.correct: A verb in the subjunctive mood.
items.why: Students use infinitive after impersonal expressions. 'Il faut que tu PARTES' (subjunctive), not 'il faut que tu partir'. The que triggers subjunctive in the following clause.

items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the passé composé?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.random_wrong: When you don't know what tense to use.
items.why: Students confuse past tenses by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: passé composé = completed event ('I went'); imparfait = ongoing/habitual/descriptive ('I used to go', 'I was going').

items.q: How do French adjectives change for feminine?
items.why: It's Add -e. “Add -s” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Most adjectives come WHERE the noun?
items.why: Correct answer: After. “Before” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many letters do you usually add to make a French adjective feminine?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
